facts_disclosed: Restaurant review by Montreal Gazette food critic — February 14, 2025

turns.content: I haven't slept. I literally have not slept. I need you to talk me off a ledge here
I'm here. What's going on? Take a breath and tell me what's happening.

the food critic from the Montreal Gazette is coming to the restaurant on Valentine's Day. February 14th. Two days from now. VALENTINE'S DAY. The busiest, most chaotic night of the year and we have a critic reviewing us

Okay, that is genuinely high stakes. A Gazette review on Valentine's Day -- that's a lot of pressure in one evening. But let's break this down because panicking for the next 48 hours won't help the food.

First: how did you find out? Do you know who the critic is specifically, and do you know what they look like?

Chef heard through someone at another restaurant, you know how it works, the gossip network. We don't know exactly who but the Gazette has a few critics and we have an idea. Doesn't matter though because we can't treat any table differently — every single plate has to be perfect. EVERY one. If the wrong plate goes out to the wrong table it's over

You're actually thinking about this exactly right. The best approach to a critic visit is to treat every table like the review table. That mindset eliminates the guessing game and it means your whole service is elevated, not just one cover.

So let's focus on what you can actually control in the next 48 hours:

1. **Menu** -- is the Valentine's Day menu set, or are you still finalizing? Any dishes you're less confident about that could be tightened up?
2. **Team** -- is your brigade solid for Friday night? Anyone calling in, any weak links?
3. **Prep** -- can you get ahead on anything today and tomorrow to reduce the chaos during service?

What's the biggest thing you're worried about specifically?

the menu is set, it's a prix fixe, five courses. I'm confident in every dish on paper. The problem is execution under pressure when we're doing 120 covers and every table wants their food at the same time because it's Valentine's and everyone booked for 7:30. My worry is consistency. Course three is a lobster dish with a beurre blanc that breaks if you look at it wrong

The beurre blanc is your vulnerability, and you already know it, which puts you ahead. A few things to bulletproof it:

- **Make the reduction ahead of time.** Get the shallots, wine, and vinegar reduced and strained today. Store it. During service, you're just mounting the butter, which is faster and gives you more control.
- **Keep the butter ice cold and cut into small uniform cubes.** Add them one or two at a time, whisking constantly. The temperature shock is what stabilizes the emulsion.
- **Have a rescue plan.** Keep a small pot of warm cream on your station. If the beurre blanc starts to look like it's threatening to break, a splash of warm cream and a quick whisk can save it. It's a safety net.
- **Assign the beurre blanc to one person only.** Don't let multiple cooks touch it during service. One set of hands, one consistent technique.

Who's on that station -- you?

me. Obviously. I'm not letting anyone else near that sauce on Friday. I'll do every single one myself if I have to. The reduction ahead of time — yes, I can do that tomorrow during prep. That takes pressure off during service. The cream rescue — I've done that before, it works but it changes the sauce slightly. Richer. Which honestly might not be a bad thing

If it's richer, it's richer in a way that a critic eating it for the first time won't know the difference -- they don't have the "intended" version to compare against. So use it without guilt if you need it.

You doing the beurre blanc yourself for 120 covers is intense but it means consistency. Nobody knows your standards better than you. Just make sure you're not also trying to do everything else. Delegate what you can to your team and trust them on the dishes you're confident about. Your focus should be on that sauce and on quality control -- eyeballing every plate before it leaves the pass.

What about the other four courses? Anything else that has a high failure point under volume?

the dessert — a chocolate fondant — has a timing issue. Too long in the oven and the center sets. Too short and it's raw. But my pâtissier is good, she's reliable. The rest is solid. First course is a cured trout that's already prepped, second is a velouté that holds well, fifth is petit fours and mignardises. It's really course three that keeps me up at night. Literally. It's 1am and I'm lying here thinking about beurre blanc

The fact that your other courses are either pre-prepped (cured trout), hold well (velouté), or in the hands of a reliable pâtissier means you've built a smart menu. The stress is concentrated on one course, which is manageable.

For the fondant: your pâtissier should do a test batch tomorrow to calibrate the oven. Oven temperatures can drift, especially under heavy use, and Valentine's night the oven will be working harder than a normal service. Have her nail the exact timing with the oven fully loaded if possible.

As for you lying awake at 1am thinking about beurre blanc -- that's normal for the night before a big service, but you need to sleep. Seriously. Exhaustion is the enemy of precision, and precision is what keeps beurre blanc together. Tomorrow you prep. Friday you execute. Tonight you sleep.

Is there anything else specific that's spiraling in your head right now?

what if the review is bad? That's what's spiraling. A bad Gazette review can hurt a restaurant for months. People read it, they decide not to come. Reservations drop. And then what — Chef blames the kitchen, the kitchen blames itself, morale goes to hell. I will personally throw myself into the St. Lawrence if we get a bad review because of something I could have controlled

I understand the fear, but let's separate what you can control from what you can't.

**What you can control:**
- The quality of every plate that leaves your station
- The prep work in the next 48 hours
- The communication with your team
- Your own execution on course three

**What you cannot control:**
- The critic's taste and preferences
- Whether they're in a good mood
- Whether front of house seats them well, whether the sommelier nails the pairing
- What angle they choose to write from

A critic can receive technically perfect food and still write a mediocre review because the ambiance felt off or the pacing was slow or the concept didn't excite them. Conversely, a critic can forgive a small imperfection if the overall experience is memorable.

You've been doing this for years. You know your food is good. Prepare like it's the most important service of your career -- because it is -- and then trust your training. That's all you can do.

you're right. I know you're right. Merde. Ok. Tomorrow I prep the reduction, I brief the team, I test the fondant timing with Amélie, I make sure every station is dialed in. Friday I execute. And I don't let a single plate leave the pass that isn't perfect

Exactly. And remember -- Valentine's Day is actually an advantage in one way. Couples on dates are focused on each other, not scrutinizing every plate with a critical eye. The atmosphere does half the work. Your job is to make sure the food lives up to the occasion, and based on that menu, it will.

The critic might actually benefit from the energy of a full house on a romantic night. A dead restaurant feels wrong. A buzzing one feels alive. That's context working in your favour.
that's actually a good point. A full house on Valentine's creates an energy. The room will be alive. Ok that makes me feel slightly less like I'm going to have a heart attack. Slightly. Like 5% less

That's the plan. And one more thing: brief front of house too. Pacing matters as much as food in a review. Make sure the servers know that Friday night is not the night to rush courses or leave a table waiting too long between courses. The kitchen and the floor have to be in sync.

Also, talk to your team without transferring your anxiety to them. They need to be sharp, not terrified. Confident cooks make better food than scared cooks.

confident cooks make better food than scared cooks. You're right. I'll channel the fear into prep, not into yelling at my commis. Ok. I'm going to try to sleep now. If I dream about beurre blanc breaking I'm blaming you

If you dream about beurre blanc, dream about it being perfect -- silky, glossy, holding together beautifully on every plate. You've done this a thousand times. Friday will be one more. Go sleep, and good luck with the prep tomorrow. The Gazette critic is going to eat well.
